Facts.text: Six residents of Carthage, North Africa, are executed for being Christian, the earliest recorded instance of Christianity in the region
John III begins his reign as Catholic Pope succeeding Pelagius I
St Leo IV dies, ending his reign as Catholic Pope; he will be succeeded by Benedict III
Arnulf III the Hapless becomes Earl of Flanders
Siege of Constantinople begins during the Fourth Crusade, as Roman Catholic Crusaders, aboard a Venetian fleet, attack the city
Pope bans Emperor Frederick II Hohenstaufen for 3rd time
Illuminated Hebrew Bible, the "Rothschild Pentateuch", signed and completed by scribes Elijah ben Meshullam and Elijah ben Jehiel (now in the Getty Museum)
Osmanen occupy Turnovo, Bulgaria
Dauphin crowned King Charles VII of France (despite having been king since 1422)
Battle of Castillon: French army beats English force under John Talbot, turning point of the Hundred Years' War
Venice recaptures nearby Italian city of Padua
Jews are expelled from Ghent, Belgium
Siena drives Spanish troops out of Verdun
Spanish and Walloon troops conquer Dunkerk
At 10:30 am, Dutch explorer Willem Barents arrives at Novaya Zemlya (Arctic Ocean)

A meeting takes place at Lüneburg between several Protestant powers in order to discuss the formation of an 'evangelical' league of defense, called the 'Confederatio Militiae Evangelicae', against the Catholic League.
Great Britain, Portugal and France sign ceasefire [or 19th]
Simon van Slingelandt appointed Dutch pension advisor
Prospero Lambertini chosen Pope Benedictus XIV
Richard Allen organizes Philadelphia's Bethel African Meth Episcopal Church
British humorous and satirical magazine "Punch" is first published; it closes in 2002
Harvard Observatory takes 1st photograph of a star (Vega)
The Great Train Wreck of 1856 between Camp Hill and Fort Washington, Pennsylvania kills over 60 people
Recovery of the bell of HMS Lutine from ship's wreck, hung from rostrum in Lloyd's of London's Underwriting Room
At Manassas, Virginia General Beauregard requests reinforcements for his 22,000 men, General Johnston is ordered to Manassas
US Congress authorizes paper money
Naval Engagement at Pascagoula River Mississippi: USS Potomac Expedition
United army officially divides corps
US army authorized to accept African-Americans as laborers
Battle of Honey Springs - largest battle in Indian Territory
Māori forces are defeated by British troops at Koheroa, Waikato, in the New Zealand Wars between Maori tribes and British colonials
CSA President Davis replaces General Joe Johnston with John Bell Hood
Italian fleet under Admiral Count Carlo Pellion di Persano captures Austrian Fort Lissa
1st US dental school, Harvard School of Dental Medicine, established

Nqwiliso, tribal chief of Western Pondoland and eldest son of Ndamase, signs a treaty with H.G. Elliott ceding sovereign rights and shipping in the Umzimvubu River mouth to the Cape government, Southern Africa
1st railroad opens in Hawaii
Arthur Shrewsbury is the first to score 1,000 runs in Test cricket
1st ship arrives in Seattle carrying gold from Yukon
Lord Tennyson, son of the poet, is named to succeed Lord Hopetown, first governor general of Australia
Orioles forfeit to St Louis having only 5 players available to play they then forfeit their franchise back to the AL
The Russian Social Democratic Workers Party meets, first in Brussels and then London because their leaders have been forced into exile by the Russian Government
Overthrown shah of Persia Mohammed Ali lands on Astrabad with army
International Amateur Athletic Federation forms in Stockholm, Sweden (now known as World Athletics)
Italian offensive at Isonzo
Longest errorless game, Cubs beat Phillies 2-1 in 21 innings
Finland adopts constitution
Yanks 21 hits, Browns 17 hits Browns win 7-6 in 17, on squeeze play
Curacao harbor workers begin strike under Felix Chacuto
Carl Mays gives up 13 runs and 20 hits in 13-0 lose to Indians
St Louis Card Jesse Haines no-hits Boston Braves, 5-0
USSR breaks diplomatic relations with China
After successfully crossing the Atlantic Ocean, the Lithuanian research aircraft Lituanica crashes in Germany under mysterious circumstances
Variety magazine publishes the famous headline "Sticks Nix Hick Pix"

Elmer Fudd, originally Egghead, is a Warner Bros. cartoon character created by Tex Avery and Chuck Jones for the Looney Tunes and Merrie Melodies series, first debuting as Egghead in "Egghead Rides Again"
Douglas "Wrong Way" Corrigan leaves New York flying for Los Angeles and winds up in Ireland supposedly by mistake
3' of rain falls on Pennsylvania, flooding kills 15
Estimated 34.5" (87.5 cm) of rainfall, Smethport, Pennsylvania (state record); flooding kills 15
Transport #6 departs Pithiviers, France with 928 French Jews sent to Auschwitz Concentration camp; 45 survive until the war's end
RAF bombs Germany rocket base Peenemunde
Soviet troops cross Bug River and march into Poland

WWII: Port Chicago Disaster - explosion during munition loading kills 322 and, injures nearly 400, destroys three ships and a pier at the Port Chicago Naval Magazine in Port Chicago, California; conditions inspire the Port Chicago Mutiny later that summer
Israeli army captures Nazareth
Proclamation of constitution of Republic of (South) Korea
Indonesian troops land on Buru, South-Molukka
Suppression of Communism Act comes into force in South Africa
Western New England College in Springfield, Massachusetts, is chartered
1st major league game where majority of team is black (Dodgers)
Construction of Disneyland commences in Anaheim, California
Theodor Heuss re-elected president of West Germany
Arco, Idaho, becomes 1st US city lit by nuclear power
Disneyland televises its grand opening in Anaheim, California
King Hussein declares himself head of Jordan/Iraqi federation
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Enewetak Atoll in the Marshall Islands
2,000 ft long by 1,300 foot wide section of ridge falls into Madis
River Canyon extending man-made Lake Hebgen by 5 miles. (Montana)
Tibet abolishes serfdom
John Chancellor becomes news anchor of "Today Show"
Robert White in X-15 sets an altitude record of 108 km (354,300 ft)
Senate rejects medicare for aged
US performs nuclear Test at Nevada Test Site
Telstar soccer team forms in Ijmuiden
Don Campbell sets a record for a turbine vehicle at 690.91 kph (429.31 mph)
Great Britain performs nuclear Test at Nevada Test Site
WLCY (now WTSP) TV channel 10 in St Petersburg-Tampa, Florida (ABC) begins
American athlete Jim Ryun sets World mile record (3:51.3) in Berkeley, California
Indians set club record by hitting 7 HR in 15-2 win over Detroit
Jimi Hendrix quits as opening act of the Monkees' tour, after playing 7 of a planned 29 shows
Race riots in Cairo, Illinois
Bloodless coup in Iraq led by General Ahmed Hassan al-Bakr
The Beatles' animated film "Yellow Submarine" premieres in London
30,000 attend Randall's Island Rock Festival, NYC
First two women begin training as FBI agents at Quantico
Military coup in Afghanistan, led by former prime minister, and cousin to the king, Mohammad Daoud Khan; King Mohammad Zahir Shah remains in Italy were he was receiving medical treatment at the time
Bob Gibson becomes second pitcher to strike out 3,000 batters (Cesar Geronimo)
France performs nuclear Test at Mururoa Atoll
The Moody Blues open 1st quadrophonic recording studio in UK
Four British soldiers are killed by a Provisional Irish Republican Army remote-controlled bomb near Forkill, County Armagh; attack the first major breach of a February truce
21st modern Olympic games open in Montreal: 25 African teams (later rising to 33 nations) boycott the games due to New Zealand playing rugby in apartheid South Africa
Simone Veil becomes chairman of European Parliament
Bolivian military coup; General Garcia Meza becomes president
Zenko Suzuki becomes Premier of Japan
Fulton County (Atlanta) grand jury indicts Wayne B William 23-year-old photographers, for murder of 2 of 28 blacks killed in Atlanta
Glasdrumman ambush: the Provisional Irish Republican Army attack a British Army post in South Armagh, killing 1 soldier and injuring another
Humber Estuary Bridge in the UK opens as the world's longest span (1.4 km)
Israeli bombers destroy PLO/al-Fatah headquarters in Beirut
Lobby walkways at Kansas City's Hyatt Regency hotel collapse killing 114 and injuring over 200
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
1st USFL championship (Mich Panthers beats Philadelphia Stars 24-22)
Pierre Mauroy resigns as Prime Minister of France
Soyuz T-12 carries 3 cosmonauts to space station Salyut 7
US passes National Minimum Drinking Age Act, prohibiting under 21's from buying or possessing alcohol as a condition of receiving federal highway funds
10 teens die in Guadalupe River flood (Comfort, Tx)
Dow Jones Industrial Average closes above 2,500 (2,510.04) for 1st time
Iran and France break diplomatic relations
Four billion TV viewers watch Mandela's 70th Birthday Tribute
Highest temperature ever recorded in San Francisco, 103°F (39°C)
1st test flight of US stealth bomber
Reds reliever Kent Tekulve retires after 1,070 appearances
Hussein's Revolutionary Day speech claims Kuwait stole oil from Iraq
Minnesota Twins become 1st team to turn 2 triple plays in a game but lose to Boston Red Sox 1-0
Slovak parliament declares independence from Czechoslovakia, beginning the so-called Velvet Divorce
Graeme Obree breaks the world hour cycling record, riding 51.596km on Old Faithful at the Hamar velodrome in Norway
A French 4-year-old boy becomes Buddhist Lama Tulku Kalou Rinpoche
Paris-bound flight TWA 800, explodes off the coast of Long Island, New York, killing all 230 on board the Boeing 747
STS 94 (Columbia 23) lands
Former South African PresidentNelson Mandelacalls for commitment by the world to take action against AIDS
TAM Airlines (TAM Linhas Aéreas) Flight 3054 crashes upon landing during rain in São Paulo. Brazil's deadliest aviation accident to date with estimated 199 deaths.
Jakarta double bombings at the JW Marriott and Ritz-Carlton Hotels killed 9 people including 4 foreigners
17 people are wounded in a bar shooting in Tuscaloosa, Alabama
58 people are killed in floods in Sichuan Province, China
7 people are killed by a car bomb in Damascus, Syria
5 hour humanitarian ceasefire, proposed by the UN, takes place between Israel and Hamas

Malaysia Airlines Flight 17 is shot down over Eastern Ukraine by a Buk surface-to-air missile launched from pro-Russian separatist-controlled territory, killing all 283 passengers and 15 crew on board
Scientists solve mystery of sleeping sickness in two villages in northern Kazakhstan - uranium mining had caused increase in carbon monoxide
Suicide bomber in Khan Bani Saad, Iraq, kills 120, ISIS claims responsibility
The Muppets Studio fires the voice of Kermit the Frog, Steve Whitmire, citing his “unacceptable business conduct”
Australian basketball center Liz Cambage scores 53 points as the Dallas Wings beat the NY Liberty 104-87 to set new WNBA single game scoring record
Bloomberg estimates NFL made $14 billion in revenue in 2017, distributing a record $8.1 billion to the league's 32 teams, $255m each
Oldest evidence of bread made from wild grains is discovered by archaeologists in a 14,000-year-old dig in the Black Desert, Jordan
Bulgaria announces 5 million people, virtually every adult, has had their personal information exposed after national tax agency hacked
Irrigation canal system collapses near Fort Laramie, Wyoming parching 100,000 acres of farmland across Nebraska and Wyoming
Streaming service Netflix reaches 150 million subscribers worldwide, but with slower growth than forecast
World Health Organization declares the DR Congo Ebola outbreak a "public health emergency of international concern"
India becomes the third country to record 1 million cases of COVID-19, 56% concentrated in Maharashtra, Delhi and Tamil Nadu
73rd Cannes Film Festival: Titane wins Palme d'Or, its director Frenchwoman Julie Ducournau only 2nd woman to win
Russia pulls out of a deal allowing the export of Ukrainian grain past their naval blockade, threatening to push world food prices higher
Typhoon Talim, makes landfall on China's Guandong coast with winds of nearly 140km/h (87mph), forcing the evacuation of nearly 230,000 people

Cavan Sullivan of the Philadelphia Union becomes the youngest player in a Major League Soccer match and the youngest to debut in any major North American professional sports league at 14 years and 293 days old in a 5-1 win over the New England Revolution at Subaru Park in Chester, Pennsylvania
The Mirage Hotel and Casino on the Las Vegas strip closes after 34 years
US Secretary of the Navy Carlos Del Toro officially exonerates all 258 Black sailors who protested work conditions after the Port Chicago Disaster in 1944; none of them are alive for the ruling
British government announces the voting age will be lowered to 16 years old across the UK in time for the next general election
Yulia Svyrydenko is appointed Prime Minister of Ukraine, succeeding Denys Shmyhal
Holy Roman Emperor Henry III crowns his sonHenry IVking
Charles the Bold, Duke of Burgundy, conquers city of Nijmegen
Martin Lutherenters a Augustinian monastery at Erfurt
English secret service discovers Anthony Babington's plot to murder plotQueen Elizabeth I
English explorerWalter Raleighis arrested by forces ofKing James Iof England
George Frideric Handel's "Water Music" premieres repeatedly on a barge cruising the River Thames in London
EmpressCatherine IIbecomes Tsarina of Russia after the death and rumored assassination ofTsar Peter III
CaptainJames Cookarrives in the New Hebrides (Vanuatu)
Members of the French National Guard under command of General Lafayette open fire on crowd of radical Jacobins at Champ de Mars, Paris, during theFrench Revolution, killing up to 50 people
Matthew Flinderspublishes "A Voyage to Terra Australis," detailing his circumnavigation of Australia, and is the first to name the continent Australia. He dies a day later.

"L'Argus" accidentally discovers a raft holding survivors from the wrecked French frigate Méduse. After 13 days at sea, only 15 of 151 remain, the rest having been cannibalized, murdered, or committed suicide. Made famous byThéodore Géricault’s painting "The Raft of the Medusa."
John Hunt Morgan's confederate cavalry raid reaches Cynthiana, Kentucky
Cecil Rhodesbecomes Premier of Cape Colony
NY GiantChristy Mathewsonbegins career losing to Bkln Superbas
Armand Fallieres is elected President of France, but power lies withGeorges Clémenceau
Ty Cobbgets 5 hits in a game for record 4th time in a year
Tris Speaker, is 5th to get 3,000 hits
Paavo Nurmiwalks world record 4x1500m (16:11.4)
Babe Ruthdraws his 2,000th base on balls at Cleveland
NY Giants future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcherCarl Hubbellbegins MLB record 24 game winning streak, beating Pittsburgh Pirates, 6-0 at Forbes Field in Pittsburgh
Spanish generalsFrancisco Francoand Emilio Mola lead a right-wing uprising, starting the Spanish Civil War
NY YankeeJoe DiMaggio's 56-game hitting streak ends in Cleveland
Shah of PersiaMohammad Reza Pahlavinames Ghavam Sultaneh premier
MGM releases film "High Society", a musical re-working of "The Philadelphia Story" set in Newport, Rhode Island; starringBing Crosby,Grace Kelly, andFrank Sinatra, with songs byCole Porter
Peter Shaffer's musical "Five Finger Exercise" premieres in London
"North by Northwest" directed byAlfred Hitchcockand starringCary Grantand Eva Marie Saint premieres in Los Angeles

PaleoanthropologistMary Leakeydiscovers the partial skull of a new species of early human ancestor, Zinjanthropus boisei or "Zinj" (now called Paranthropus boisei), which lived in Africa almost 2 million years ago
Dave DeBusscherehits a single off Bennie Daniels, his 1st and only MLB hit
ANC leaderNelson Mandela, recently sentenced to life imprisonment, is awarded the Joliot Curie Gold Medal for Peace
John Lennonis again ordered to leave the US in 60 days due to a 1968 marijuana charge in the UK (he doesn't)
NASA's Apollo 18 and SovietSoyuz 19make 1st US/USSR linkup in space
Indonesian PresidentSuhartoannexes East Timor
Walter Alstonbecomes the fifth MLB manager to win 2,000 games as Los Angeles Dodgers defeat visiting Chicago Cubs, 5-2
NY Yankee managerBilly MartinandReggie Jacksonfight in dug out after Jackson refuses to bunt, causing Martin to suspend him
Nicaraguan dictatorAnastasio Somoza Debayleflees to Miami
Sebastian Coeruns world record 3:49 mile in Oslo
Ronald Reaganformally accepts Republican nomination for US president
New York YankeesDon Mattinglyis 2nd to hit HRs in 7 straight AL games (en route to 8)
Florence Griffith Joynerof the USA sets 100 m woman's record (10.49)
Paul McCartneyreleases single "This One", from his album "Flowers In The Dirt"
NY YankeeDeion Sandershits an inside park homer
Hulk HoganbeatsRic Flairto win WCW wrestling championship
Forbes Magazine announcesBill Gatesis the richest man in world with a net worth of $12.9 billion dollars
Singer-songwriterCarole King's 1971 album "Tapestry"  is certified as Diamond for sales of 10 million units in the US
"The Mask of Zorro" directed by Martin Campbell, starringAntonio Banderas,Catherine Zeta-JonesandAnthony Hopkinsis released
Russia buries TsarNicholas IIand family, 80 years after they died
Atlantic Records releases English singer-rapperCraig David's debut album “Born to Do It” in the United States
13th ESPY Awards:Lance Armstrong,Annika Sörenstamwin
FIFA Women's World Cup Final, Commerzbank-Arena, Frankfurt, Germany: Japan beats US, 3-1 on penalties; 2-2 after extra time
21st ESPY Awards:LeBron James,Serena Williamswin
U2singerBonois made a Commandeur of the French Ordre des Arts et des Lettres
Marvel's "Ant-Man", starringPaul Ruddas the titular character, is released
17 works by Swiss architectLe Corbusierincluded in UNESCO World Heritage sites list as "an Outstanding Contribution to the Modern Movement"
Barack Obamagives speech honoringNelson Mandelaand warning of "strongman politics" in Johannesburg, South Africa
CBS announces the cancellation of "The Late Show WithStephen Colbert" after running since 2015
Spanish–American War: Spaniards surrender to US forces at Santiago de Cuba
Royal Proclamation by KingGeorge Vchanges name of British Royal family from German Saxe-Coburg-Gotha to Windsor
The Romanov royal family and several of their retainers are executed by a Bolshevik firing squad in the basement of Ipatiev House in Yekaterinburg, Russia
LeadersHarry Truman, Joseph Stalin and Winston Churchill hold their first post World War II meeting at the Potsdam Conference
US Air Force pilot Gail Halvorsen encounters children in at Templehof Airport in Berlin during the Berlin Blockade, giving him the idea to drop candy in 'Operation Little Vittles'
Mexican drug cartel headJoaquín "El Chapo" Guzmánsentenced to life in prison plus 30 years in New York
Ismail I, Shah of Persia, converted Iran from Sunni to Shi'ah, born in Ardabīl, Iran (d. 1524)
Isaac Watts, English writer, preacher and hymnist (Horae Lyrican), born in Southampton, England (d. 1748)
Johann Schneider, German composer and pupil of Bach, born in Coburg, Duchy of Saxe-Coburg (d. 1788)
Elbridge Gerry, American statesman who invented gerrymandering and 5th Vice President of the United States, born in Marblehead, Massachusetts Bay (d. 1814)
Johannes Hendricus van der Palm, Dutch theologist and Minister of Education, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1840)
August Harder, German composer, born in Schönerstädt, Electorate of Saxony (d. 1813)
Hippolyte Delaroche, French painter, born in Paris (d. 1856)
Ignace Leybach, French pianist and composer, born in Gambsheim, Alsace, France (d. 1891)
Frederick Able, English chemist and inventor (cordiet), born in Woolwich, England (d. 1902)
Xianfeng, Emperor of the Manchu-led Qing dynasty, born in Old Summer Palace, Beijing, Qing dynasty, China (d. 1861)
August Söderman, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1876)
Ephraim Shay, American inventor of Shay type of geared steam locomotive, born in Huron, Ohio (d. 1916)
Friedrich Gernsheim, German composer, born in Worms, Grand Duchy of Hesse und bei Rhine (d. 1916)
Tokugawa Iemochi, 14th Japanese shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate (1858-66), born in Minato, Japan (d. 1866)
Luis Muñoz Rivera, Puerto Rican journalist (founded Federalist Party), born in Barranquitas, Puerto Rico (d. 1916)
James Henry Whittaker, English-New Zealand founder of artisan chocolate company Whittaker's in 1896, born in Macclesfield, England (d. 1947)[1]
Lyonel Feininger, German-American cartoonist and painter, born in New York City (d. 1956)
Donald Tovey, British musicologist (Essays in Musical Analysis), pianist, and composer (The Bride of Dionysus), born in Eton, England (d. 1940)

Maxim Litvinov [Meir Genoch Moiseyevich Wallach], Russian statesman, revolutionary and diplomat (People's Commissar for Foreign Affairs.1930-39, Soviet Ambassador to the US 1940-43), born in Białystok, Russian Empire (d. 1951)
Rosa Jackson Lumpkin, lived to be 115 years old, born in Flint, Georgia (d. 1991)
Vittorio Gnecchi, Italian composer (Cassandra), born in Milan, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1954)
Henri Zagwijn, Dutch composer and musicologist, born in Nieuwer-Amstel, Netherlands (d. 1954)
Bart de Ligt, Dutch pacificist and anti-militarist (Acting Peace), born in Schalkwijk, Netherlands (d. 1938)
Mauritz Stiller [Moshe Stiller], Swedish actor and director (The Saga of Gösta Berling, Erotikon), born in Helsinki, Finland (d. 1928)
Benjamin Dale, British composer (Before the Paling of the Stars; The Flowing Tide), and educator (Royal Academy), born in Upper Holloway, Islington, England (d. 1943)
Shmuel Agnon, Israeli writer (Day Before Yesterday, Nobel Prize for Literature 1966), born in Buczacz, Polish Galicia (d. 1970)[1]
Joe Dawson, American auto racer (Indianapolis 500 1912), born in Odon, Indiana (d. 1946)
Mary Clare, British actress (Evil Mind, Young & Innocent), born in London, England (d. 1970)
Georges Lemaître, Belgian Catholic Priest, astronomer and physics professor, who initially proposed the universe expansion and big-bang theories, born in Charleroi, Belgium (d. 1966)
Elbert Parr Tuttle, American lawyer, judge and civil rights champion (one of the Fifth Circuit Four), born in Pasadena, California (d. 1996)[1]
Berenice Abbott, American photographer (The World of Atget; Changing New York), born in Springfield, Ohio (d. 1991)
G. Robert Vincent, American sound recording pioneer (Edison Laboratories; Armed Forces Radio Service; Nuremberg Trials; National Voice Library), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1985)
Osmond Borradaile, Canadian cinematographer, known for "The Four Feathers", born in Winnipeg, Manitoba (d. 1999)
Bruno Jasieński, Polish poet and writer, leader in Polish Futurist movement and communist, born in Klimontów, Poland (d. 1938)
Arnold Pihlak, Estonian soccer forward (44 caps; FK Austria Wien), born in Tallinn, Estonia (d. 1985)
Christina Stead, Australia, novelist (Man Who Loved Children), born in Rockdale, Australia (d. 1983)
Valerian Mikhaylovich Bogdanov-Berezovsky, Russian Soviet composer, born in Starozhilovka, Russia (d. 1971)
Jef Alpaerts, Flemish pianist, educator, and conductor (Collegium Musicum Antverpiense), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1973)
Bill Gargan, American actor (The Bells of St. Mary's; You Only Live Once), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1979)
John Carroll [Julian LaFaye], American actor (Flying Tigers, Go West), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1979)
(Alfons) "Fud" Candrix, Belgian jazz saxophonist, violinist, and bandleader, born in Tongeren, Belgium (d. 1974)
Jack Douglas [Douglas Crickard], American humorist (My Brother Was an Only Child), and comedy writer (The Jack Paar Show; Laugh-In), born in Lynbrook, New York (d. 1989)
Rudolf Petzold, German-English educator and composer, born in Liverpool, England (d. 1991)
Hardy Amies, English royal dressmaker (Queen Elizabeth II), born in London, England (d. 2003)
Yang Jiang, Chinese writer (Six Chapters From My Life ‘Downunder,’), born in Beijing (d. 2016)
Art Linkletter, Canadian-American radio and TV host (People are Funny), born in Saskatchewan, Canada (d. 2010)
Bertrand Goldberg, American architect known for Marina City, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1997)
Everett Helm, American music critic and composer, born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1999)
Roger Garaudy, French philosopher and communist (convicted for Holocaust denial), born in Marseille, France (d. 2012)
Eleanor Steber, American soprano (Metropolitan Opera, 1940-66), born in Wheeling, West Virginia (d. 1990)
James Purdy, American writer (The House of the Solitary Maggot), born near Fremont, Ohio, (d. 2009)
Lucille Benson, American actress (Halloween II; 194;1 Bosom Buddies), born in Scottsboro, Alabama (d. 1984)
Catherine "Cass" Daley, American actress (Star Spangled Rhythm, Red Garters), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1975)
Dorothy Poynton-Hill, American diver (Olympic gold 10m platform 1932, 36; silver springboard 1928, bronze 1936), born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 1995)
Esther Williamson Ballou, American organist, composer and educator, born in Elmira, New York (d. 1973)
Habib Rajab, South African trader and collector of oriental art who fought against a takeover of the Grey Street complex by White traders, born in Durban, South Africa (d. 1973)
Kenan Evren, Turkish soldier and politician, (President of Turkey 1980-1989 by military coup), born in Alaşehir, Manisa Province (d. 2015)
Lou Boudreau, American Baseball HOF shortstop (8 × MLB All-Star; World Series & AL MVP 1948; Cleveland Indians) and manager (Cleveland, Boston RS, KC A's), born in Harvey, Illinois (d. 2001)
Carlos Manuel Arana Osorio, Guatemalan army colonel and President of Guatemala (1970-74), born in Barberena, Guatemala (d. 2003)
Alex Moir, New Zealand cricket spin bowler (17 Tests, 28 wickets, BB 6/155; Otago), born in Dunedin, New Zealand (d. 2000)
Bill Monroe, American newscaster (NBC-TV, Congressional Report), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 2011)
Esperanza Baur, Mexican film actress, and John Wayne's second wife, born in Mexico City (d. 1961)
Helen Walker, American actress (Brewster's Million; Lucky Jordan), born in Worcester, Massachusetts (d. 1968)
Juan Antonio Samaranch, Spanish sports administrator (7th President International Olympic Committee 1980-2001), born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 2010)
Nils Bohlin, Swedish mechanical engineer and inventor of the three-point seat belt, born in Härnösand, Sweden (d. 2002)
Rudolf Kárpáti, Hungarian fencer (Olympic gold sabre team 1948, 52, 56, 60; individual 1956, 60; World C'ship gold x 7), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1999)
Acquanetta [Mildred Davenport], American actress nicknamed "The Venezuelan Volcano" (Tarzan & Leopard Woman), born in Newberry, South Carolina (d. 2004)
František Zvarík, Slovakian stage actor (Slovak National Theatre), born in Vrútky, Slovakia (d. 2008)
George Barnes, American swing-jazz and session guitarist, and electric guitar pioneer, born in South Chicago Heights, Illinois (d. 1977)
Mary Osborne, American jazz guitarist (Dizzy Gillespie, Billie Holiday, Coleman Hawkins, Thelonious Monk), born in Minot, North Dakota (d. 1992)[1]
Donald Davie, English Movement poet and literary critic, born in Barnsley, Yorkshire, England (d. 1995)[1]
John Cooper, English race car designer (Cooper Car Company; developed the "Mini"), born in Surbiton, England (d. 2000)
Édouard Carpentier, French-Canadian pro wrestler (NWA 1956-57, WWA heavyweight champion), born in Roanne, Rhône-Alpes, France (d. 2010)
Jean Casadesus, French concert pianist, born in Paris (d. 1972)
Jean Muir, British fashion designer and dressmaker, born in London (d. 1995)
Joe Morello, American jazz drummer (Dave Brubeck Quartet - "Blue Rondo à la Turk"), born in Springfield, Massachussets (d. 2011)
Vince Guaraldi, American jazz pianist and composer ("Cast Your Fate to the Wind"; Peanuts TV specials), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1976)
Roy McMillan, American baseball infielder, coach and manager (MLB All Star 1956-57; Gold Glove 1957-59; Cincinnati Reds), born in Bonham, Texas (d. 1997)
Sergei Godunov, Russian mathematician, born in  Moscow, Russian SFSR, USSR (d. 2023)
Ryōhei Hirose, Japanese composer who incorporated both traditional and western instrumentation, born in Hakodate, Hokkaidō, Japan (d. 2008)
Bobby Leonard, American Basketball Hall of Fame coach (ABA C'ship 1970, 72, 73; Indiana Pacers) & point guard (Minneapolis/LA Lakers, Chicago Packers/Zephyrs), born in Terre Haute, Indiana (d. 2021)
Hal Riney, American advertising executive, born in Seattle Washington (d. 2008)
Joanne Gilbert, American actress (The Great Man, High Cost of Loving), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2025)[1]

Johnny Kerr, American basketball forward (NBA All-Star 1956, 59, 63; Syracuse Nats/Philadelphia 76ers), coach (Chicago Bulls, Phoenix Suns) and broadcaster (Chicago Bulls), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2009)
Niccolo Castiglioni, Italian composer, born in Milan, Italy (d. 1996)
Quino [Joaquín Salvador Lavado Tejón], Argentine cartoonist (Mafalda), born in Mendoza, Argentina (d. 2020)
Wojciech Kilar, Polish classical (Missa Pro Pace), and film score composer (Bram Stoker's Dracula; The Pianist), born in Lwów, Second Polish Republic (d. 2013)
Bruce Wells, English boxer (European amateur light middleweight champion 1953) and actor (Dr. Who 1965-73), born in Harlesden, Middlesex (d. 2009)
James Lloydovich Patterson, Soviet-American child actor (Circus) and poet (Chronicle of the Left Hand), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 2025)[1]
Tony Pithey, Rhodesian cricket batsman (17 Tests for South Africa; top score 154, 1965), born in Mutare, Zimbabwe (d. 2006)
Lucio Tan, Chinese-born Filipino businessman (head of Philippine Airlines and Asia Brewery Inc.), born in Amoy, China
Philippe Capdenat, French classical composer, born in Bordeaux, France
Rainer Kirsch, German writer, playwright (Heinrich Schlaghands Höllenfahrt), and poet, born in Döbeln, Germany (d. 2015)
Willie Ito, American animator for Disney Studios ("Lady and the Tramp" spaghetti kiss); Warner Brothers; Hanna-Barbara), born in San Francisco, California
